failure_modes.text: A familiar solution path can block better alternatives because the first successful frame becomes hard to unsee.
failure_modes.source_quote: An existing or previously learned solution prevents an individual from perceiving alternative solutions.
failure_modes.mitigation: Before committing to the first solution that works, explicitly search for at least one alternative; a familiar path can block better options.

failure_modes.text: Expertise can become a trap when people assume they have seen this problem before and stop testing other frames.
failure_modes.source_quote: Experienced problem solvers often rely on mental models that have worked previously, but these frames can be misleading for genuinely novel problems.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge the assumption that this is a known problem: test whether the current case actually matches the conditions where the old solution succeeded.

failure_modes.text: Anti-fixation rhetoric becomes waste when teams reject all prior patterns instead of identifying which pattern is stale and why.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the team treats avoid fixation as a reason to reject all prior knowledge.
failure_modes.mitigation: Distinguish stale patterns from useful ones: anti-fixation rhetoric that rejects all prior experience is as wasteful as blind repetition.

premortem_questions.text: What if the reason this answer arrived so fast is that we are reusing a familiar template rather than seeing the actual structure?
premortem_questions.source_quote: When a person begins thinking about a problem in one direction, that fixation can blind them to the full range of alternatives.

premortem_questions.text: What different frame, analogy, or outside-domain lens would expose a solution path we are currently missing?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Avoiding the Einstellung effect means testing multiple frameworks.

premortem_questions.text: What part of our prior knowledge is genuinely useful here, and what part is just making the current case look familiar?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The danger is allowing one’s existing toolkit to dictate the solution.

heuristics.text: Interrupt the dominant template before committing when the problem feels novel despite a suspiciously familiar answer.
heuristics.source_quote: The model is most useful when an experienced team keeps reaching for the same proven frame even though the situation feels structurally different.

heuristics.text: Switch modes or step away briefly when focused effort is reinforcing the same stale path.
heuristics.source_quote: The path to overcoming the effect is to turn the focus away from the subject.

heuristics.text: Keep helpful pattern recognition, but force a fresh test of whether this case actually satisfies the old frame's assumptions.
heuristics.source_quote: Danger when the team treats avoid fixation as a reason to reject all prior knowledge instead of distinguishing helpful pattern recognition from stale pattern lock-in.

curation_notes.summary: Kept the chunks on stale-template interruption, cross-frame testing, and anti-overcorrection discipline.
donor_drops: Did not preserve broader educational-method sprawl beyond the clearest de-fixation moves.
open_questions: The source supports learning-technique applications, but the Wave 2 surface stays on stale-problem-frame lock-in.

allies: The raw source is strongest alongside first-principles-thinking, lateral-thinking, and step-back style interruption.
antagonists: The source pushes hardest against confirmation-bias, availability-heuristic, and familiar-tool lock-in.
structured_tensions: The clearest explicit tension is with schema-acquisition when templates harden into stale defaults.
